text: The man who is not corrupted by the love of money or the desire for power, but who devotes himself entirely to the service of the state, deserves the highest honor.
context: Urging Athens to resist Philip of Macedon before it was too late.

text: You have no idea what is coming. You are blind to the danger that stands at your gates. The time for deliberation is past; the time for action is now.
context: A direct warning to the Athenian assembly about Macedonian aggression.

text: It is not by the number of ships or the weight of treasure that wars are decided, but by the courage of those who fight for freedom.
context: Emphasizing resolve over material resources in the face of tyranny.

text: If we continue to sit idle and take no thought for the morrow, we shall find that we have no choice left but to do unwillingly what we might have done of our own free will.
context: On the cost of inaction against an expanding power.

text: A man who is a slave to pleasure and luxury can never be a true friend to the people or a faithful guardian of the state.
context: Defending his career and attacking his rival Aeschines.

text: The strongest fortress is not made of stone and timber, but of the goodwill and courage of free citizens.
context: Contrasting Athenian liberty with Macedonian military power.

text: It is the duty of a good citizen to prefer the safety of his country to his own ease and reputation.
context: Part of Demosthenes' justification for his anti-Macedonian policy.

text: We must not measure our danger by the number of the enemy, but by the justice of our cause and the spirit of our people.
context: Encouraging Athenians despite Philip's growing army.

text: The orator who flatters the people prepares their ruin; the one who tells them hard truths is their true friend.
context: Critique of demagogues who told Athenians only what they wanted to hear.

text: Freedom is not preserved by words alone, but by the readiness to fight and suffer for it.
context: Reflecting on the sacrifices required to oppose Philip.

text: Philip knows well that while Athens remains free, his own power can never be secure.
context: Analyzing Philip's strategic interest in subduing Athens.

text: It is better to die a thousand times than to live in slavery and dishonor under a foreign master.
context: A call to arms and moral resistance.

text: The past cannot be undone, but the future is still in our hands if we have the courage to grasp it.
context: Defending policy decisions while looking forward.

text: An army of deer led by a lion is more to be feared than an army of lions led by a deer.
source: Attributed in various accounts of his oratory
context: Emphasizing the importance of leadership quality.

text: We are not contending for a strip of land or a harbor, but for the very existence of our city and the freedom of Greece.
context: Framing the conflict in existential terms.

text: The man who has never been tested by adversity cannot know the strength of his own soul.
Delay has been the ruin of many states; prompt and vigorous action has saved them.
If you will not fight for your freedom when the enemy is still far off, you will fight for it when he is at your doors, and fight in vain.
The voice of a free people, united in purpose, is more powerful than the weapons of any tyrant.
I have never sought popularity by saying what was pleasant rather than what was true.
context: Proud declaration of his political integrity.

text: The man who is the slave of his passions is the most wretched of all beings.
It is the mark of a great man to be able to bear the reverses of fortune with equanimity.
A city that is well governed is the best defense against its enemies.
He who is not able to control himself is not fit to command others.
The greatest glory is not in never falling, but in rising every time we fall.
We must not be discouraged by the magnitude of the task, but encouraged by the justice of our cause.
The true orator is the one who can make the truth prevail over falsehood.
A free people cannot be enslaved except by their own consent.
It is better to suffer in a good cause than to prosper in a bad one.
The voice of the people, when united, is the voice of God.
He who would be free must strike the first blow.
The safety of the state is the highest law.
context: On priority of the common good.

text: Words without deeds are like clouds without rain.
The man who fears not death is the master of all men.
We fight not for glory but for the preservation of our freedom.
A wise man learns from the mistakes of others; a fool from his own.
The power of the orator lies not in his voice but in the truth he speaks.
When the people are united, no tyrant can stand against them.
It is the duty of every citizen to defend the laws as he would defend his own life.
The greatest enemy of the people is not the foreign invader but the traitor within.
He who has truth on his side need not fear the multitude.
The orator's art is to make the just cause appear the stronger.
A nation that values its freedom will not hesitate to defend it with arms.
The memory of great deeds is the immortality of the statesman.
We must prepare for war if we wish to preserve peace.
The true patriot is he who serves his country in its darkest hours.
Speech is the mirror of the soul; as a man speaks, so he is.
No man is so foolish as to prefer war to peace, but some are so foolish as to prefer dishonor to war.
The orator who sways the multitude sways the destiny of the state.
We are all Greeks; our common heritage binds us against the barbarian.
To endure is the first lesson of the statesman.
The people are the best judges of their own interests when they are not deceived.
A single act of courage can save a nation.
The orator's words are the seeds from which the actions of the people spring.
We must not barter our freedom for a false security.
The highest virtue is to serve the state without thought of self.
When danger threatens, he who hesitates is lost.
The orator who speaks the truth, though it be bitter, is the true friend of the people.
A people that is not ready to fight for its liberty does not deserve to be free.
The memory of our ancestors calls upon us to be worthy of them.
The orator's power is measured not by the applause he receives but by the good he does.
We must arm ourselves with courage as our ancestors armed themselves with spears.
The traitor is more dangerous than the open enemy.
He who loves his country will not spare himself in its defense.
The orator who can move the hearts of men can move the world.
In the hour of peril, the true man stands forth.
We must not allow the enemy to dictate the terms of our existence.
The orator's tongue is the sword of the state.
A free state is the only state worth living in.
The man who betrays his country betrays humanity.
We must be prepared to pay the price of liberty.
The orator who wins the people wins the future.
Courage is the foundation of all virtue.
The state that neglects its orators neglects its soul.
We fight today so that our children may live free tomorrow.
The orator's duty is to speak for those who cannot speak for themselves.
A people united in purpose is invincible.
The truth will prevail if it is spoken with courage.
We must not yield an inch of our ancestral freedom.
The orator who stirs the blood of the people stirs the destiny of nations.
In the defense of liberty, no sacrifice is too great.
The man who stands alone for the right is stronger than the multitude in error.
We must look the danger in the face and meet it with united hearts.
The orator's voice is the trumpet that calls the people to their duty.
A nation is only as strong as the virtue of its citizens.
He who would lead must first learn to follow the truth.
The struggle for freedom is the noblest of all struggles.
The orator who wins without truth wins only a hollow victory.
We are the heirs of a glorious past; let us not be the authors of a shameful future.
The orator's art is the art of making men see what they would rather not see.
Liberty is the breath of life to a free people.
The man who betrays the trust of the people is the worst of criminals.
We must not rest until the last enemy of our freedom is vanquished.
The orator who speaks from the heart moves the hearts of others.
A free people will always find the means to defend their freedom.
The orator's duty is to the truth, not to the applause of the moment.
We stand or fall together; there is no middle way in the defense of liberty.
The memory of the fallen calls us to finish the work they began.
The orator who can unite a divided people is the greatest of statesmen.
In the end, it is not the strength of our arms but the justice of our cause that will prevail.
